Children.Text: VeloxDev is a suite of development tools for modern .NET, centered around Source Generators, which unifies previously fragmented capabilities—such as MVVM, animation, theming, frame loops, AOP, Workflow, and Agent—into a single cohesive abstraction. It advocates composition over inheritance, type safety, high extensibility, and elegant APIs.
Select and install any GUI framework you prefer; all packages reference `VeloxDev.Core`, and `VeloxDev.Core.Extension` is optional.

Cells: Decorate fields with [VeloxProperty] and methods with [VeloxCommand] to automatically generate properties and asynchronous commands
State interpolation animations: Snapshot recording, Fluent stitching, cross-platform scheduling
[ThemeConfig] declares multiple themes, supporting gradual transitions and runtime overrides
Update / FixedUpdate frame loops, global time flow and pausing
Runtime proxying: before/after/override hooks for properties and methods
Generate reflection-preserving code for specified types to handle trimming and NativeAOT
Tree / Node / Slot / Link + Helper graph-structured workflow core
Zero-dependency reflection toolkit + MAF Function Calling toolkit

Children.Text: Focus on practical application to quickly master how to use the project.
VeloxDev provides featured ViewModel completion and Command generation, compatible with mainstream MVVM frameworks in the community, and avoids redundant generation of infrastructure.

Children.Code: [VeloxProperty] private string name = string.Empty;
[VeloxProperty] public partial string Name { get; protected set; }

Children.Text: If a member is derived from `INotifyCollectionChanged`, additional callback methods are provided. Specifically, when the value of a notifying property changes, callbacks are automatically subscribed to and unsubscribed from internally. Note that partial properties cannot declare initial values at definition time; therefore, we recommend initializing these collections during construction.
The following code will all ultimately generate a `SaveCommand`, regardless of whether it carries the “Async” suffix.

No matter how you execute the command, the underlying mechanism is always waiting on a Task; therefore, in most cases, you don’t need to worry that calling `Execute` instead of `ExecuteAsync` will block the UI — they are nearly equivalent.

Children.Code:
SaveCommand.Execute(null);

await SaveCommand.ExecuteAsync(null);

Children.Text: Command cancellation is also available in two versions; here we demonstrate the waitable version.
When you set `canValidate: true`, the internal `CanExecuteSaveCommand` callback will be generated, and you can determine whether to allow command execution in the callback function.

The UI can be manually notified to re-evaluate the command’s executability, at which point the `parameter` is `null`; if the command is executed, the `parameter` is the argument received by the command.

Children.Code: SaveCommand.Notify();

Children.Text: .NET 5+ natively supports proxy-based aspect-oriented programming, meaning dynamic interception and customization of property and method behavior can be achieved without modifying the type’s source code; VeloxDev provides配套 source code generation for this.
When setting up a proxy, there are five key parameters.
Use these parameters in the `SetProxy` method.

Cells: Parameters received at the entry point, e.g., the `value` keyword in properties or parameters retrieved during method invocation
Parameters returned in the previous lifecycle

Children.Text: Hooks only take effect when properties are accessed through a proxy; in real-world applications, you can choose as needed.
Frame loop behavior is commonly used in scenarios such as physics simulation and state monitoring. Now, you can build it using an attribute.
You can create multiple groups based on the `channel` parameter and specify the refresh rate using the `fps` parameter.
Basic control can be implemented based on these static functions.

Children.Code:
MonoBehaviourManager.Start("game"); // Start

MonoBehaviourManager.Pause("game"); // Pause (not equivalent to stop)

MonoBehaviourManager.Resume("game"); // Resume

await MonoBehaviourManager.StopAsync("game"); // Stop

Children.Text: The time scale and refresh rate can be modified at any time.

Children.Code:
MonoBehaviourManager.SetTimeScale(0.5f, "game"); // Time scale

MonoBehaviourManager.SetTargetFPS(30, "game"); // Frame rate

bool isRun = MonoBehaviourManager.IsRunning("game"); // Checks whether the "game" MonoBehaviour is currently running

bool isPaused = MonoBehaviourManager.IsPaused("game"); // Checks whether the "game" MonoBehaviour is currently paused

Cells: Time elapsed since the previous frame

Children.Text: Almost any GUI framework provides proprietary interfaces for handling animations, which makes the creation of interpolation-based animations somewhat dependent on the GUI framework’s syntactic design—and may impose limitations. Take WPF as an example: standard animations must be built upon `DependencyProperty`. Although this design choice is intended to facilitate internal performance optimization, it also sacrifices flexibility.

VeloxDev advocates compatibility and internally designs a complete abstraction layer for animations—from construction to execution—so that each GUI framework requires only minimal adaptation code, and ultimately animations operate on standard properties rather than any GUI-specific objects.
Theme — Vivid Theme Color Switching Implementation
You can use the same API to build animations in any supported GUI framework—we provide the most elegant approach to date, with strong emphasis on type safety and thread safety.
In the following example, you will create an animation that modifies the horizontal offset and fill color of a Rectangle element.
The animation process can be split into multiple segments and then concatenated in sequence.

Children.Code:
Transition<Rectangle>.Create()
    .Property()
    .Effect()
    .Then()  // Chain: execute the next animation immediately
    .Property()
    .Effect()
    .AwaitThen() // Chain: wait for a specified time before executing the next animation
    .Property()
    .Effect();

Children.Text: Use the following code to control the animation toggle.

Children.Code:
animation.Execute(rect); // Start the animation on the rect instance

Transition.Exit(rect); // Terminate

Children.Text: Animation startup is thread-safe; you need not worry about the following scenario.

The `IInterpolable` interface allows types to participate directly in interpolation. By implementing this interface, your custom types can be used directly with the `Property` function when building animations in Fluent.
The `IEaseCalculator` interface allows you to customize easing effects.

Animations are based on interpolation between two states; the snapshot mechanism is one of its implementations. VeloxDev exposes this mechanism, enabling you to use it for creating animations—or for state saving and restoration (used only in animation scenarios).

Children.Code:
// The essence is still animation; effects can be configured.
snapshot.Effect(TransitionEffects.Empty).Execute(view);

Children.Text: This is an extended capability derived from the animation system, enabling natural transitions between one UI theme color scheme and another.
If you configure an interpolator, the following functions become available.
Of course, considering that gradient-style transitions may cause performance issues in some scenarios, a non-animated approach is also provided—this method is supported at all times.

Process-oriented and automated workflows are a critical capability metric in modern software development—especially as AI technologies advance, building, configuring, and executing complex graphs will no longer be a daunting task (Intelligent Agents support is listed separately in the Table of Contents). In this Getting Started section, the table in the documentation lists only commonly used members; for complete details, refer to the APIs.
Using the VeloxDev workflow framework, you will experience:
Based on MVVM, with no GUI dependencies
Agent dynamically understands and operates workflows
A workflow is a graph structure containing four key components. This chapter will show you how to build the view models corresponding to these components.

Cells: Global container that stores all data for a single workflow
Node that hosts configuration and business logic execution
Connector that hosts connection relationships and channel direction constraints
Connection line that can access the source and destination Slot

Children.Text: Next, all view models will be built based on the following attributes: `$component` is the component type, and `$helper` is the injected business logic.

Children.Code: 【WorkflowBuilder.{$component}<{$helper}>】

Children.Text:
| Member                                             | Type                                                                                               | Description              |
| -------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ |
| `VirtualLink`                                    | `IWorkflowLinkViewModel`                                                                         | Virtual connection during connection setup |
| `Nodes`                                          | `ObservableCollection<IWorkflowNodeViewModel>`                                                   | All Node components      |
| `Links`                                          | `ObservableCollection<IWorkflowLinkViewModel>`                                                   | All Link components      |

| Member                                             | Type                                                                                             | Description              |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------ |
| `CreateNodeCommand`                              | `IVeloxCommand`                                                                                  | Create node command      |
| `SetPointerCommand`                              | `IVeloxCommand`                                                                                  | Set pointer position command |
| `ResetVirtualLinkCommand`                        | `IVeloxCommand`                                                                                  | Reset virtual link command |
| `RedoCommand`                                    | `IVeloxCommand`                                                                                  | Redo command             |
| `UndoCommand`                                    | `IVeloxCommand`                                                                                  | Undo command             |

In the definition phase of a node view model, there are two special members:  
- A class derived from `IWorkflowSlotViewModel`, which must be annotated with `[VeloxProperty]`.  
- A `SlotEnumerator<T>`, which can use `bool` or `enum` as conditional branches; internally, the collection generates multiple slots based on the condition and must also be annotated with `[VeloxProperty]`.  

With this setup, you can later directly render multiple connectors in the view using the `InputSlot` property and the `OutputSlots` collection. Note that the UI is responsible for tracking the slot coordinates (`Anchor`) and passing them back to the view model. If the UI lacks such coordinate tracking, the start and end positions of your `Link` component cannot be computed correctly.

`SlotEnumerator<T>` implements the `IEnumerable` interface, with the following child elements defined: `Name` is the name of the enumeration member, `Value` is the enumeration value, and `Slot` is an automatically constructed `Slot` component.

| Member                                             | Type                                             | Description          |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | -------------------- |
| `Parent`                                         | `IWorkflowTreeViewModel?`                      | The associated Tree  |
| `Anchor`                                         | `Anchor`                                       | Node anchor coordinates |
| `Size`                                           | `Size`                                         | Node size            |
| `Slots`                                          | `ObservableCollection<IWorkflowSlotViewModel>` | Collection of Slots owned by the node |

| Member                                               | Type                                             | Description                 |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------- |
| `MoveCommand`                                      | `IVeloxCommand`                                | Move node command         |
| `SetAnchorCommand`                                 | `IVeloxCommand`                                | Set anchor command        |
| `SetSizeCommand`                                   | `IVeloxCommand`                                | Set size command          |
| `CreateSlotCommand`                                | `IVeloxCommand`                                | Create slot command       |
| `DeleteCommand`                                    | `IVeloxCommand`                                | Delete node command       |
| `WorkCommand`                                      | `IVeloxCommand`                                | Execute work command      |
| `BroadcastCommand`                                 | `IVeloxCommand`                                | Forward broadcast command |
| `ReverseBroadcastCommand`                          | `IVeloxCommand`                                | Reverse broadcast command |

| Member                                             | Type                                             | Description                |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------- |
| `Targets`                                        | `ObservableCollection<IWorkflowSlotViewModel>` | Collection of targets the current Slot points to |
| `Sources`                                        | `ObservableCollection<IWorkflowSlotViewModel>` | Collection of sources received by the current Slot |
| `Parent`                                         | `IWorkflowNodeViewModel?`                      | Parent Node                |
| `Channel`                                        | `SlotChannel`                                  | Slot channel type          |
| `State`                                          | `SlotState`                                    | Slot connection state      |
| `Anchor`                                         | `Anchor`                                       | Slot anchor position       |

| Member                                               | Type                                             | Description                       |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------- |

| `SendConnectionCommand`                          | `IVeloxCommand`                                | Send connection command           |

| `DeleteCommand`                                  | `IVeloxCommand`                                | Delete slot command               |

| `Receiver`                                       | `IWorkflowSlotViewModel`       | Connection receiver |
| `IsVisible`                                      | `bool`                         | Whether the connection is visible |

The logic of all workflow components is injectable and replaceable, and events and overridable methods are provided to allow customization. Each workflow component you define holds a `Helper` property, through which you can access the business implementation.
After using the generic version, all built-in Component properties of Helper will become concrete derived classes; the Component property in the non-generic version is in interface abstract form.

Cells: Provides extended behaviors for the global container
Provides extended behaviors for connection lines

If you have already read the sections on component construction and business logic injection, the workflow is now ready to run.

If you wish to build a GUI for it, start by reading this chapter—I’ve extracted many critical code snippets from Avalonia’s official examples as a reference pattern. The content focuses on:  
    - Command invocation  
    - Canvas layout  
    - Virtualization adaptation  
    - Data binding
💡 The author’s energy is extremely limited. If you have a better GUI implementation, feel free to exchange ideas!

In Avalonia, you can store data templates for components collectively in `<UserControl.DataTemplates />`; Avalonia automatically selects the appropriate template based on the `DataType`. For workflows, the two most critical components are nodes and connections. In the following example, pay close attention to its data binding—for instance, which crucial data `NodeView` binds from `NodeViewModel`.

Children.Code:
<UserControl.DataTemplates>
		<!-- Node template -->
		<DataTemplate DataType="vm:NodeViewModel">
			<local:NodeView Width="{Binding Size.Width}"
							Height="{Binding Size.Height}"
							Canvas.Left="{Binding Anchor.Horizontal}"
							Canvas.Top="{Binding Anchor.Vertical}"
							ZIndex="{Binding Anchor.Layer}"
							RenderTransform="{Binding $parent[local:WorkflowView].CanvasTransform}"/>
		</DataTemplate>
		
		<!-- Connection line template -->
		<DataTemplate DataType="vm:LinkViewModel">
			<Panel>
				<local:BezierCurveView StartLeft="{Binding Sender.Anchor.Horizontal}"
									   StartTop="{Binding Sender.Anchor.Vertical}"
									   EndLeft="{Binding Receiver.Anchor.Horizontal}"
									   EndTop="{Binding Receiver.Anchor.Vertical}"
									   CanRender="{Binding IsVisible}"
									   IsVisible="{Binding !UsePolyline}"
									   Width="{Binding $parent[Canvas].Bounds.Width}"
									   Height="{Binding $parent[Canvas].Bounds.Height}"
									   RenderTransform="{Binding $parent[local:WorkflowView].CanvasTransform}"
									   ZIndex="-1"/>
			</Panel>
		</DataTemplate>
	</UserControl.DataTemplates>

Children.Text:
The Tree Helper has built-in virtualization support; when using data templates, you can bind to `Helper.VisibleItems`.

If you prefer not to enable virtualization (which requires manually calculating the viewport and passing the frame object back to the framework—a more complex task), you can instead bind to `Tree.Nodes` and `Tree.Links`.

`ViewPool` is an attached property that provides object pooling support for `Canvas` (not provided by the library itself—only present in the Demo).

Children.Code:
<!-- Render node editor (virtualization support) -->
<Grid Grid.Column="2">
	<Border BorderBrush="White" BorderThickness="1" CornerRadius="3"
			PointerPressed="OnCanvasPointerPressed">
		<local:WorkflowGridDecorator x:Name="Root_GridDecorator">
			<ScrollViewer HorizontalScrollBarVisibility="Auto"
						  ScrollChanged="OnScrollChanged"
						  x:Name="Root_ScrollViewer">
				<Canvas x:DataType="vm:TreeViewModel"
						x:Name="Root_Canvas"
						Width="{Binding Layout.ActualSize.Width}"
						Height="{Binding Layout.ActualSize.Height}"
						HorizontalAlignment="Left"
						VerticalAlignment="Top"
						local:ViewPool.ItemsSource="{Binding Helper.VisibleItems}">
					<Canvas.DataTemplates>
						<!-- Search for data templates by priority: Self > Parent > App -->
					</Canvas.DataTemplates>
				</Canvas>
			</ScrollViewer>
		</local:WorkflowGridDecorator>
	</Border>
</Grid>

Children.Text: CanvasLayout is a critically important class that provides the capability to record the overall canvas layout at the data level, track canvas expansion status, and update the theoretical canvas offset. You may optionally define corresponding notification properties in the Tree component.
The table below provides a detailed description of the members’ purposes.

Cells: The size of the canvas when it was created
The positive offset: when the canvas needs to extend its length to the right or downward, this property is updated first
The negative offset: when the canvas needs to extend its length to the left or upward, this property is updated first
The current actual size of the canvas
The theoretical offset of the canvas content

Children.Text:
In summary, you need to:  
- Calculate the overscroll amount of the `ScrollViewer` during scrolling in its UI backend code, and update `PositiveOffset` and `NegativeOffset` accordingly based on the overscroll direction.  
- Bind the `Canvas`’s `ActualSize` to obtain its size.  
- Bind all child elements inside the `Canvas` to the same `TranslateTransform`, which is constructed from the theoretical offset provided by `ActualOffset`.

If you choose to obtain Node/Link components from the `Helper.VisibleItems` collection during content rendering, your UI must track the `ScrollViewer`’s current viewport and pass it back to the view model, as shown in the following code.

Based on various Pointer events, drag-and-drop logic for nodes can be implemented. Of course, you can also leverage community-provided Behavior libraries to quickly achieve this; the key is to invoke `MoveCommand` and pass in the offset.
We recommend that you read the “Components” section of the workflow documentation. The definition of `Slot` is special: a `Node` must internally track the coordinates of its `Slot`.
Execute only the connection initiation and reception commands provided inside the `SlotViewModel` when a `Pointer` event occurs; the workflow framework handles everything internally.
The Link component currently has no special interaction; you can easily draw lines in the GUI by using decorators or geometric shapes.

VeloxDev.Core.Extension provides a set of extension methods for view models, with the following features:  
- `Dictionary<,>` supports any type as the key, no longer limited to `string`.  
- Only properties whose setter and getter are both `public` are serialized, and serialization handles only properties.

| Target / Scenario | Configuration Syntax | Why This Configuration | Recommended? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Debugging, viewing JSON, performing diffs | `SerializationOptions.Create().WithIndented().WithTypeNameHandling(TypeNameHandling.Auto).WithNullValueHandling(NullValueHandling.Include).WithDefaultValueHandling(DefaultValueHandling.Include)` | Maximizes JSON readability and fully preserves polymorphism, null values, and default values — ideal for diagnosing workflow restoration issues | Strongly recommended |
| Production saving to file | `SerializationOptions.Create().WithCompact().WithTypeNameHandling(TypeNameHandling.Auto).WithNullValueHandling(NullValueHandling.Include).WithDefaultValueHandling(DefaultValueHandling.Include)` | Produces smaller files while retaining all information required for full workflow persistence | Recommended |
| Minimal JSON size only | `SerializationOptions.Create().WithCompact().WithNullValueHandling(NullValueHandling.Ignore).WithDefaultValueHandling(DefaultValueHandling.Ignore)` | Reduces JSON size but may omit fields critical for restoration — unsuitable for complex workflow object graphs | Use with caution |
| Derived nodes, interface properties, or polymorphic objects | `WithTypeNameHandling(TypeNameHandling.Auto)` | Enables deserialization to restore the actual runtime type; typically required in Workflow scenarios | Required |
| Exact state restoration after deserialization | `WithNullValueHandling(NullValueHandling.Include).WithDefaultValueHandling(DefaultValueHandling.Include)` | Preserves `null` values and default values, preventing state divergence post-deserialization | Recommended |
| Improving JSON readability only | `WithIndented()` | Improves human readability only; does not alter object semantics | As needed |
| Reducing file size only | `WithCompact()` | Removes indentation and whitespace — simplest compression method | As needed |
| Synchronous API usage | `var json = tree.Serialize(options);` / `var tree = json.Deserialize<TreeViewModel>(options);` | Suitable for simple save/load operations | Recommended |
| Safe loading (e.g., from untrusted sources) | `json.TryDeserialize<TreeViewModel>(options, out var tree)` | More robust when loading from potentially corrupted external files or user input — avoids throwing exceptions directly | Strongly recommended |
| Asynchronous API usage | `await tree.SerializeAsync(options)` / `await json.DeserializeAsync<TreeViewModel>(options)` | Suitable for UI, CLI commands, and file I/O pipelines | Recommended |
| Streaming / Stream API usage | `await tree.SerializeToStreamAsync(stream, options)` / `await stream.DeserializeFromStreamAsync<TreeViewModel>(options)` | Suitable for file streams, network streams, and memory streams | Recommended |
| Most robust default for Workflows | `SerializationOptions.Create().WithIndented().WithTypeNameHandling(TypeNameHandling.Auto).WithNullValueHandling(NullValueHandling.Include).WithDefaultValueHandling(DefaultValueHandling.Include)` | Use this if uncertain — safest for workflow object graphs | Default choice |

Based on MAF’s Function Calling, the repository starts from C# objects and progressively provides agent-ready tools, ultimately evolving into workflow systems and other functionality modules suitable for agent empowerment. In the end, developers only need to annotate members in their code using natural language; the agent can then dynamically understand and modify in-memory objects at runtime.

# VeloxDev Agent Core Capabilities Summary

## 1. Document Purpose

This document summarizes only the core Agent-related capabilities in VeloxDev. These capabilities target generic .NET objects and are not tied to any specific business scenario.

---

## 2. Capability Scope

These capabilities fulfill the following responsibilities:

- Define metadata representation for Agents  
- Provide multilingual context description capability  
- Provide reflection-based discovery capability  
- Provide access and execution capability for properties, methods, and commands  
- Wrap arbitrary objects into toolsets consumable by AI  

They serve as foundational infrastructure for the VeloxDev Agent system.

---

## 3. Core Module Distribution

### 3.1 Metadata and Language System

Located primarily under `Src/Core/VeloxDev.Core/AI/`.

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `AgentContextAttribute` | Provides semantic descriptions for types, properties, fields, methods, and commands |
| `AgentCommandParameterAttribute` | Marks the expected type of command parameters |
| `AgentLanguages` | Defines supported language enumeration |
| `AgentLanguagesExtensions` | Provides language code parsing, display name conversion, and related utilities |

These types establish a unified descriptive language for Agents, enabling not only “reflectability” but also “explainability”.

---

### 3.2 Context Reading Capability

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `AgentContextReader` | Reads `AgentContextAttribute` information from types or members |

This module extracts semantic annotations written by developers in source code for use by downstream tooling or prompt engineering layers.

---

### 3.3 Property Access Capability

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `AgentPropertyAccessor` | Discovers, reads, sets, and batch-patches properties |

Key capabilities include:

- Enumerating publicly accessible properties of an object  
- Returning property type, readability/writability status  
- Retrieving current property values  
- Setting individual property values  
- Performing bulk patch operations on multiple properties  
- Performing basic type conversions and enum value conversions  

This is one of the foundational mechanisms for Agents to modify object state.

---

### 3.4 Method Invocation Capability

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `AgentMethodInvoker` | Discovers public methods and executes method calls |

Key capabilities include:

- Filtering out property accessors and base `object` methods  
- Outputting method signatures, parameter lists, and return types  
- Handling basic overload resolution  
- Supporting optional parameter filling  
- Attempting common parameter type conversions  
- Supporting both instance and static method invocation  

This is a critical foundational component when Agents execute business logic.

---

### 3.5 Command Discovery and Execution Capability

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `AgentCommandDiscoverer` | Discovers `ICommand` properties on objects and executes associated commands |

Key capabilities include:

- Enumerating available commands on an object  
- Reading command parameter types  
- Reading command semantic descriptions  
- Determining whether a command is currently executable  
- Executing commands by name  
- Supporting automatic suffix completion (e.g., `Command`)  
- Inferring associated commands from property names  

This capability is especially important for MVVM-style objects.

---

### 3.6 Type Resolution Capability

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `AgentTypeResolver` | Resolves .NET types within currently loaded assemblies |

This capability supports:

- Resolving types by full name  
- Scanning loaded assemblies if `Type.GetType` fails  
- Providing type resolution support for method invocation, command parameter construction, and tool output generation  

---

### 3.7 Tool Call Notification Capability

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `AgentToolCallEventArgs` | Represents event data after a tool call |
| `IAgentToolCallNotifier` | Defines the tool call notification event interface |

This capability enables:

- Recording the invoked tool name  
- Capturing the tool’s return result  
- Tracking the number of tool calls within the current session  
- Providing a traceable entry point for upper-layer systems  

---

## 4. Generic Object Tooling Capability

Located at `Src/Core/VeloxDev.Core.Extension/Agent/AgentObjectToolkit.cs`.

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `AgentObjectToolkit` | Wraps any object into a set of `AITool` instances |
| `AgentObjectToolkitExtensions` | Provides extension methods to simplify toolkit instantiation |

### 4.1 Exposable Tool Capabilities

`AgentObjectToolkit` wraps an object into the following tools:

- `GetComponentInfo`  
- `ListProperties`  
- `GetProperty`  
- `SetProperty`  
- `PatchProperties`  
- `ListCommands`  
- `ExecuteCommand`  
- `ListMethods`  
- `InvokeMethod`  
- `ResolveType`  

### 4.2 Value Proposition

The value of this tooling layer lies in:

- Requiring no framework-specific inheritance  
- Requiring no base class derivation  
- Enabling Agent usage of any object with public properties, methods, or commands  
- Uniformly converting reflection capabilities into AI Runtime-consumable tool interfaces  

---

## 5. Structural Layering of Core Capabilities

Core capabilities can be grouped into four layers:

| Layer | Content |
|---|---|
| Metadata Layer | `AgentContextAttribute`, `AgentCommandParameterAttribute`, `AgentLanguages` |
| Discovery Layer | `AgentContextReader`, `AgentPropertyAccessor`, `AgentMethodInvoker`, `AgentCommandDiscoverer`, `AgentTypeResolver` |
| Execution Layer | Property setting, method invocation, command execution, type conversion |
| Tooling Layer | `AgentObjectToolkit`, `AgentObjectToolkitExtensions` |

---

## 6. Characteristics of Core Capabilities

### 6.1 High Generality

- Targets arbitrary .NET objects  
- Not bound to any single UI framework  
- Not bound to any specific business model  

### 6.2 Low Coupling

- Separation between metadata and execution logic  
- Separation between reflection discovery and AI tool wrapping  
- Separation between infrastructure and business scenarios  

### 6.3 Extensibility

Future extensions may include:

- New object tool wrappers  
- New type description exporters  
- New parameter binding strategies  
- New tool call auditing capabilities  

---

## 7. Applicable Scenarios

These core capabilities are suitable for:

- Agent-enabling standard ViewModels  
- General-purpose component debugging and exploration  
- AI-assisted object inspection and invocation  
- Building reflection-based tooling systems  
- Multilingual semantic description–driven Agent integration  

---

## 8. Final Summary

VeloxDev’s Agent core capabilities constitute a set of general-purpose infrastructure built around four pillars: **describing objects**, **discovering objects**, **invoking objects**, and **tooling objects**.

Their core value does not lie in any particular business use case, but rather in:

- Making code structure understandable to Agents  
- Making object capabilities discoverable by Agents  
- Enabling safe runtime invocation of objects by Agents  
- Uniformly packaging arbitrary objects as AI toolsets
Wrap ordinary objects into Agent-operable ones

# VeloxDev Workflow Capability Summary

## 1. Document Purpose

This document summarizes only the Workflow capabilities in VeloxDev, including its domain model, default implementations, runtime mechanisms, and Agent-facing operational support.

---

## 2. Workflow Capability Positioning

VeloxDev’s Workflow capability targets visual workflow editing scenarios, with core objectives including:

- Defining data models for workflow trees, nodes, slots, and links  
- Providing node layout and spatial management capabilities  
- Providing runtime behaviors such as connection, broadcast, and reverse broadcast  
- Providing undoable/redoable edit history  
- Providing dynamic node, dynamic slot, and enum slot management capabilities  
- Providing Agent-facing workflow querying, operation, analysis, and takeover capabilities  

---

## 3. Workflow Core Domain Model

Primarily located in `Src/Core/VeloxDev.Core/WorkflowSystem/` and `Interfaces/WorkflowSystem/`.

### 3.1 Core Interfaces

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `IWorkflowViewModel` | Generic base interface for Workflow components |
| `IWorkflowTreeViewModel` | Workflow tree or canvas root object |
| `IWorkflowNodeViewModel` | Workflow node object |
| `IWorkflowSlotViewModel` | Workflow slot object |
| `IWorkflowLinkViewModel` | Workflow link object |
| `IWorkflowTreeViewModelHelper` | Behavior-driving helper interface for Tree |
| `IWorkflowNodeViewModelHelper` | Behavior-driving helper interface for Node |
| `IWorkflowHelper` | General-purpose helper interface |
| `IWorkflowIdentifiable` | Provides runtime identification capability |

These interfaces collectively define the core object boundaries within the Workflow system.

---

### 3.2 Core Data Types

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Anchor` | Node position and hierarchy information |
| `Offset` | Node offset |
| `Size` | Node dimensions |
| `Viewport` | Viewport or spatial query range |
| `WorkflowActionPair` | Action pair used for Undo/Redo |
| `CellKey` | Spatial grid location key |
| `CanvasLayout` | Canvas layout information |

These data types fulfill spatial representation and operational payload responsibilities in Workflow.

---

### 3.3 Default Implementation Templates

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `TreeViewModelBase` | Default implementation of `IWorkflowTreeViewModel` |
| `NodeViewModelBase` | Default implementation of `IWorkflowNodeViewModel` |
| `SlotViewModelBase` | Default implementation of `IWorkflowSlotViewModel` |
| `LinkViewModelBase` | Default implementation of `IWorkflowLinkViewModel` |

These base classes provide out-of-the-box default workflow component models, suitable as foundational templates for business node types.

---

## 4. Workflow Basic Runtime Capabilities

### 4.1 Tree-Level Capabilities

`IWorkflowTreeViewModel` and its default implementation assume the following responsibilities:

- Maintaining the collection of all nodes (`Nodes`)  
- Maintaining the collection of all links (`Links`)  
- Maintaining the connection mapping (`LinksMap`)  
- Maintaining virtual links during connection interactions (`VirtualLink`)  
- Creating nodes  
- Setting pointer position  
- Initiating and receiving connection establishment  
- Committing operation history  
- Supporting undo and redo  

This is the core container of the entire Workflow editor.

---

### 4.2 Node-Level Capabilities

`IWorkflowNodeViewModel` and its default implementation assume the following responsibilities:

- Maintaining the node’s `Anchor`  
- Maintaining the node’s `Size`  
- Maintaining the node’s owned `Slots`  
- Providing move command  
- Providing set-position command  
- Providing set-size command  
- Providing create-slot command  
- Providing delete-node command  
- Providing execute-work command  
- Providing broadcast command  
- Providing reverse-broadcast command  

The node is the most fundamental business-bearing unit in Workflow.

---

### 4.3 Slot and Link Capabilities

Slots and Links jointly fulfill graph-connection structure construction:

- A Slot represents an input/output connection point on a node  
- A Link represents the actual connection between two Slots  
- A Slot maintains source and target collections  
- A Link expresses the sender–receiver relationship  
- The Tree uses `LinksMap` to maintain topology mappings efficiently  

---

## 5. Workflow Extension Mechanisms

### 5.1 Standard Extension Modules

`WorkflowSystem/StandardEx/` provides several standard extension capabilities, e.g.:

- `WorkflowTreeEx`  
- `WorkflowNodeEx`  
- `WorkflowSlotEx`  
- `WorkflowLinkEx`  
- `WorkflowCommandEx`  
- `WorkflowSpatialEx`  

These extensions typically supplement convenient APIs for default workflow components.

---

### 5.2 Template Helper Modules

`WorkflowSystem/Templates/Helpers/` provides:

- `TreeHelper`  
- `NodeHelper`  
- `SlotHelper`  
- `LinkHelper`  

These Helpers encapsulate behavioral logic in default implementations, such as:

- Node creation  
- Slot creation  
- Link establishment and validation  
- Deletion logic  
- Undo/Redo submission  
- Broadcast and reverse-broadcast execution  

---

### 5.3 Spatial and Visualization Support

Workflow also provides spatial management capabilities, e.g.:

- `WorkflowSpatialManager`  
- `SpatialGridHashMap`  
- `NodeBoundsProvider`  
- `LinkBoundsProvider`  

These modules support:

- Spatial indexing  
- Viewport-based queries  
- Node collision or bounding-range checks  
- Link and node boundary calculations  

---

## 6. Workflow Agent Operational Support

Located primarily in `Src/Core/VeloxDev.Core.Extension/Agent/Workflow/`.

### 6.1 Entry Points and Scoping

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `AgentEx` | Provides the `AsAgentScope()` entry point |
| `WorkflowAgentScope` | Manages the current Workflow Agent’s scope, context, and resources |

`WorkflowAgentScope` provides:

- Holding the current Tree  
- Registration of custom components, interfaces, enums, and data types  
- Registration of custom tools  
- Registration of skills, reference materials, and script resources  
- Full and progressive context output  
- Tool invocation counting and callback management  

---

### 6.2 Type Context Capabilities

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `AgentContextCollector` | Generates Agent-readable contextual documentation for Workflow types |
| `AgentEmbeddedResources` | Reads embedded Markdown skills and reference materials |

These capabilities equip Agents with necessary type awareness and operational hints upon entering a workflow editing scenario.

---

### 6.3 State Tracking Capabilities

| Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `WorkflowStateTracker` | Generates workflow snapshots and outputs node/link deltas |

It supports:

- Capturing a snapshot of the current tree state  
- Computing differences between two snapshots  
- Returning newly added, deleted, and modified nodes  
- Returning newly added and deleted links  
- Assigning monotonically increasing version numbers to each snapshot  

Suitable for Agents maintaining workflow state awareness across multi-turn conversations.

---

### 6.4 Parameter Protocol Models

`WorkflowProtocols.cs` defines a set of protocol objects for expressing Agent operation parameters, e.g.:

- `MoveNodeProtocol`  
- `SetNodePositionProtocol`  
- `ResizeNodeProtocol`  
- `DeleteNodeProtocol`  
- `ConnectSlotsProtocol`  
- `DisconnectSlotsProtocol`  
- `ExecuteWorkProtocol`  
- `PatchPropertiesProtocol`  
- `GetTypeSchemaProtocol`  
- `ExecuteCommandProtocol`  
- `CreateNodeProtocol`  
- `CreateSlotProtocol`  

These protocol objects enable strongly-typed expression of Workflow operation parameters.

---

## 7. Capabilities Provided by WorkflowAgentToolkit

`WorkflowAgentToolkit` is the core toolkit implementation for Workflow Agents.

### 7.1 Query Capabilities

- `ListNodes`  
- `GetNodeDetail`  
- `GetNodeDetailById`  
- `ListConnections`  
- `GetLinkDetail`  
- `GetWorkflowSummary`  
- `GetComponentContext`  
- `ListComponentCommands`  
- `GetFullTopology`  

Help Agents understand the current workflow structure.

---

### 7.2 Mutation Capabilities

- `MoveNode`  
- `SetNodePosition`  
- `ResizeNode`  
- `DeleteNode`  
- `DeleteSlot`  
- `PatchNodeProperties`  
- `PatchComponentById`  
- `CreateNode`  
- `CreateSlotOnNode`  
- `CreateAndConfigureNode`  
- `DeleteNodes`  

Perform modifications to the workflow graph structure and node states.

---

### 7.3 Connection Management Capabilities

- `ConnectSlots`  
- `ConnectSlotsById`  
- `DisconnectSlots`  
- `DisconnectSlotsById`  
- `DisconnectAllFromSlot`  
- `DisconnectAllFromNode`  
- `ReplaceConnection`  
- `ConnectByProperty`  

Build and adjust topological relationships among nodes.

---

### 7.4 Execution Capabilities

- `ExecuteWork`  
- `BroadcastNode`  
- `ReverseBroadcastNode`  
- `ExecuteCommandOnNode`  
- `ExecuteCommandById`  
- `ExecuteWorkOnNodes`  
- `BatchExecute`  

Drive node behavior and component commands.

---

### 7.5 Slot Collection and Enum Slot Capabilities

- `ListSlotProperties`  
- `AddSlotToCollection`  
- `RemoveSlotFromCollection`  
- `SetEnumSlotCollection`  
- `GetEnumSlotByValue`  
- `SetEnumSlotChannel`  
- `ConnectEnumSlot`  
- `SetSlotChannel`  

Handle dynamic slots, enum-driven slots, and slot collection management.

---

### 7.6 Search, Analysis, and Layout Capabilities

- `FindNodes`  
- `ResolveSlotId`  
- `SearchForward`  
- `SearchReverse`  
- `SearchAllRelative`  
- `IsConnected`  
- `FindPath`  
- `GetNodeStatistics`  
- `ListCreatableTypes`  
- `ValidateWorkflow`  
- `AlignNodes`  
- `DistributeNodes`  
- `AutoLayout`  
- `ArrangeNodes`  

Support topological search, statistical analysis, and automated layout.

---

### 7.7 History and State Capabilities

- `TakeSnapshot`  
- `GetChangesSinceSnapshot`  
- `Undo`  
- `Redo`  

Enable state tracking and edit rollback.

---

## 8. Workflow Capability Characteristics

### 8.1 Graph-Oriented

Workflow’s core is not single-object manipulation, but rather:

- Node collections  
- Slot collections  
- Link topology  
- Propagation relationships among nodes  

### 8.2 Editor-Oriented

It does not merely express data structures—it directly serves:

- Visual editing  
- Link construction  
- Spatial movement and layout  
- Undo/Redo history management  
- Node execution and broadcasting  

### 8.3 Extensibility- and Customization-Oriented

It allows developers to extend:

- Custom node types  
- Custom slot types  
- Custom enum slots  
- Custom context descriptions  
- Custom Agent tools and embedded resources  

---

## 9. Applicable Scenarios

VeloxDev Workflow capabilities are suitable for:

- Visual workflow editors  
- Node-based logic orchestration systems  
- Data-flow / control-flow diagram designers  
- Rule orchestration and execution graph systems  
- Graphical business designers requiring undo/redo support  
- Workflow scenarios requiring Agent takeover or assisted editing  

---

## 10. Final Summary

VeloxDev’s Workflow capability is a complete system built around “workflow graph modeling, editing, execution, analysis, and takeover.”

Its core value lies in:

- Defining a unified workflow component model  
- Providing default implementations and behavior-assist layers  
- Supporting topology connections, spatial layout, and history rollback  
- Supporting advanced constructs like dynamic slots and enum slots  
- Supporting Agent-facing workflow querying, operations, and full takeover
